params.text:
Recording your concerns

Good recording supports everything that follows. As soon as you safely can, write down what you saw, heard and were told, using clear, factual language and the person’s own words where possible. Note the date, time and place, and describe the facts rather than your opinions. Follow your organisation’s procedures for storing this information securely and sharing it only with the right people.

  • Be factual: record what you observed, not what you assume.
  • Be specific: include dates, times, places and exact words.
  • Be secure: store and share records as your policy requires.

Remember: recognise and report

You have learned what modern slavery is, the forms it takes, the signs that someone may be a victim, and how to respond safely. The heart of it is simple: modern slavery is a serious crime and a safeguarding issue that is hidden all around us, so stay alert, keep people safe, and report any concern without delay.

This course has given you awareness and knowledge to help you recognise and report — it does not replace your safeguarding policy or the supervised training you receive, and specialist agencies carry out the investigation. Never confront a suspected trafficker. Report to your manager or safeguarding lead, use the Modern Slavery Helpline for advice, and call 999 in an emergency. Your noticing could change, or even save, a life.
